speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 24th of March, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So how were you treated by the guards. You were talking before about William Tell. Can you remember any of the other guards at all, or how they treated you in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. One of the women guards used to come in and you know, she looked at the beds. The beds had to be flat. Doesn't matter how much luggage or whatever you put under-- under your blanket, but it had to be one straight line. And if it wasn't, she pulled the whole thing off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get punished?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the washing, you did your washing. It could hang during the nights above your beds, but during the day nothing was to be seen. What you did with it, under your blanket, as long as it was flat, one straight line.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kinds of punishments were there if you got things wrong?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was hanged.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stealing from the kitchen, what my friend did, too. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a great risk then to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the circumstance of the hanging? Could you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know. Just happened, and we didn't see it. But it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it held up as an example?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other kinds of punishments were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Standing on roll call for hours and hours and hours. Sick people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about with the barracks? You were saying that you each had a bed to yourself. Did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning. Later we had to share.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people would there be in one barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never counted them. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a feeling of being cramped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the beginning. But later when the war started turning a bit, and they emptied camps, and they went walk about, and more and more people came in the camp. And they had to double up. And at the very end, you know, husbands and wives, they were together in Bergen-Belsen, but not in the same barracks, of course. They had male and female barracks. And then at the end, you know, the couples said, well, if we have to share with strangers, we might as well share with our husbands. You know, they didn't care anymore at the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Before we get to that, because that sounds like a very different sort of situation from what you experienced most of the time--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of the fact that your conditions were somewhat better than the other people in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We tend to believe it, because we knew we were going to a special camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by a special camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because of the British nationality. We knew it was something different. But sometimes a German would come from the higher up, and we had to come and roll call, and some people were called out, and they had to go to the Auschwitz camps. You know, the revised papers. And like I was a born, myself, in Scotland. So that was all right for them. But then you had, one of the parents was born there, and they revised that and they had to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, one of the parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: If one of the parents were born in England, the parents were British, and for a while they regarded the children also. But later on when they revised it, the children had to go. So that happened a few times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were saying that there wasn't actually any contact with the other prisoners in Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside the camp, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you aware that there was resentment, maybe, towards the people in your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the other people in the other areas weren't living in such, in similar conditions, and were hungrier.

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know what was there. Later on we got some friends moving in in our camp. And they still did the makeup and the lipstick. But when they got dressed in the morning, there were long johns. It was not combination.

speaker: interviewer
text: How important was your appearance to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't care. Did not care. I don't think I've ever seen a mirror, to tell you the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything at that time that helped you to get through it, like the relationship with your brother? Was that something which gave you any kind of comfort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but see, he was extra hungry because he wasn't quite normal. And he went into the garbage. And somebody put him in, and he got 24 hours in the brig, they called it, in prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just isolated by himself. And I couldn't bring him any food because the man in the tower would have seen that. And I think then when he got pneumonia, and that gave him, you know, that did it, being on his own.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, that did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's when he died. You know? That helped him along. It was no good for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did he actually die?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just before, let me think, we came there in January. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '43, uh, '44. 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like for you when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible. I was called. One of the women said, you have to come and help. Go to [NON-ENGLISH] bed. Let's help him tidy his bed. His bed is in a mess. And I went there. He was dying. And so, when he died, I walked behind him. You know, they had a horse and cart, and you can walk behind it until the gates.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he in a coffin?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they don't put a coffin. But some of the men said prayers for him. And then he goes out of the gate and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there much death in the camp at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, it happened all the time. Happened all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it something that you were used to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say all the time--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, I wasn't used to it. Not until later when I started working, as a-- help out as a nurse. Then I remember when I saw my first dead person. I saw a pair of feet sticking out of the bed. And I know. But later on it didn't do anything to me. I helped them lay off, put them in the bathroom until they got them away. I had a cousin of my mother there, and she was dying. She wouldn't eat. I made her a slice of bread. I made it like a bit of cake. I put jam on it and a little figure, [INAUDIBLE] look like a little cake, trying to feed her. But she wouldn't, she had no will. And one morning I came and the other nurses said she died, and we left her for you to lay off. And it didn't do anything to me, dead people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think at the time about how before it would have affected you, and now--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by that stage, the numbness had really set in. So you were saying that you worked in the hospital. How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I became ill, typhoid. All I remember is that I wasn't in my own barrack. And one night, I had to go to the toilet so badly. And it was a terrible weather. And you had to go outside. And I remember I went outside, and then I remember that somebody picked me up and said, oh, it's Lotte, it's Lotte. And they took me to the hospital. And I was unconscious. And that's friend, you know, the mother of the boy, she brought me soup everyday. And vaguely, I remember it, and the typhoid, I have to eat. I have to eat. And I came out of it. And as soon as I was better, as they say, I was, I had to go to another barrack where I was used to, and I couldn't even get up the steps, I was so weak. Anyhow, I went a few times back to the hospital and I helped out. And I stayed there to help out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you help out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, take temperatures, get them the pan, the bed pan, and just be there for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of illnesses did people have in hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end, you know, you get big water in the legs-- fluid, fluid. Typhoid, of course, and malnutrition-- anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what kind of treatment did they have, and what did you get, apart from getting a bit of extra food from your friend? Did you have needles and that sort of thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all got a needle against typhoid, after which we all got typhoid. So I don't know what they gave us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how clean were the needles, obviously?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all got typhoid after we got the needles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that was done on purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably. Probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, you know, when I helped out in the hospital, I saw a good friend of [NON-ENGLISH], while I nursed his mother in the other ward. And that was terrible. A lot of friend had typhoid. He survived it. That was a terrible time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you help these people as much as you could?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I had to wash them. And the lice. We had so much lice. You know, everyday, we got-- we got undressed, we looked at our clothes and tried to kill, and kill with your fingers. We thought we'll never, you know, we'll do this for our lives. We never though, you know, it would get any better. Because I remember, I was washing one woman, and I had a washer in my hand, and I put it on her body and there was thousands. It was full, the washer was full. And that was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like for you? Did you have lice as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, of course

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any way that you could fight them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in terms of hygiene and washing and that sort of thing? Did that help?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wouldn't help. Wouldn't help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this time, did you think that you had any chance of surviving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't think. [LAUGHS] We did not think. As long as was alive, you was alive. I remember in the hospital, a poor mother sitting near the bed of a little child who was dying. You know? Little things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you grow attached to any of the people that you were looking after in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the doctors who were there. Were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were-- our doctors. They did what they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how competent were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were good doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have much contact with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way would you talk to them, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, conversations, but yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you were saying before that conditions got worse at the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Overcrowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's actually when the lice set in, with the overcrowding. Two, three in a bed. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other kinds of changes were there in terms of food and that sort of thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same. It was the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were these people, these new people that came into the camp, where were they from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From other camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of nationalities were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dutch, French.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what news did they bring from the other camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't know anything because they came out of worse camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you about those camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Sorry. Terrible. I'm not a good talker.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have much contact with these people at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Another day we had to leave the camp. We went out and others came in. And we tried to ask them how, you know so and so, you know so and so? And bang, bang, bang. They were hit, because we're not allowed to have contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were hit by the guards.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not us, but the others. Because they came from worse camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you yourself never experienced anything too bad from the guards in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Not-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And over the time that you were in Belsen, who organized the running of the camp? Was it the guards, or did Jewish people have positions of power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me how that worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was Jewish people, like they had one camp [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the boss of the whole camp was Greek, with [NON-ENGLISH]. And he was responsible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about within the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one woman in our camp, the barrack [INAUDIBLE]. She would wake us up in the morning and share the food out. She had to stay in the camp, in the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like when the food was doled out? Was that something very important to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. That was-- it was the news of the day. It was the best thing of the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you very concerned with how much food you would get?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody got one thing full, that was it. And as I said, if there was left, you got one number, you got a second portion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at the end, when all the people came into the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same. They still got some.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what other kinds of changes were there in terms of hygiene then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that got worse and worse, of course. As I said, the lice, millions.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about water? Was there water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, in the wash house, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to wash yourself as much as you had been in the past?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we did every morning. You know, we tried to be very hygienic ourselves. We had a full body wash every morning. Cold, cold water, soap that wouldn't foam. But we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So are there any other things that stand out in your mind from this time in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I can think of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any attempt to keep up cultural activities or religion observance?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were, yeah. There were people who kept the Friday night, on top of the bed, saved a piece of bread from during the week. And put a sheet on like a white tablecloth and make a Shabbat. Some people, because there were rabbis, very religious people. But the rabbis had to pick and shuffle their work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other jobs apart from the laundry and the shoe and the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, because I was there only from 7 January until April-- January '44 until say, March. And in the meantime, I had the typhus. There wasn't really much time. As I said, [INAUDIBLE] made us brass bags. And some people make brass of them, and some people threw them in the fire, in the potbelly, you know, just the heater. And just when they came in, when the Germans came in, we were all working. And I got into trouble with the other people there because I did too much. Because I'm somebody, what I do, I'm always very fast with everything. And they thought I did too much. I was too fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean? Why did they mind that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they went to [INAUDIBLE], you know? Do as little as possible, of course. And I did too much. And they pour in some, and they made brass with some.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do anything with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But would they have caught us, I would have gone just same, because I was in the group, whether I did it or no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was there a trust, though, amongst the lot of you that you knew that you wouldn't ever turn each other in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think we would.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the friend that brought you the soup when you were in the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever help her in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she didn't need any help.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she get the soup, though, for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how she did it, but she did. She did. Because her husband was also a cook. She was just working in the kitchen, and her husband worked as a-- maybe he got permission. I don't know. But even though, you know, they say when you're in a coma, talk to the people. They can hear it. I believe that, because vaguely, I knew she was there. And I was thinking myself, as long as I eat, I have to eat to survive. I was thinking that while I was semi-- and apparently it did help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was an instinct to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to make it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it something almost, that you didn't even think of consciously?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe not. Now I've always heard, you know, when you're sick in the camps, as long as you can eat. It must been my subconscious.

words.text: as long as you can eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So towards the end, what did you start to hear of what was going on in terms of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't hear anything. We didn't hear anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear any bombs or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, later on when we were in the train, we heard it all. I'll talk about the train later.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So when did you actually leave the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: About March, I think it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me about that? What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew we had to go. Where to, we don't know. We had to pack up. And people starting to leaving. And there was one kapo, and I said, please look after my girlfriend. She's in that bed over there. But I had my own people to look after. And I didn't go. I stayed until one of the doctor's wives said, come on, come on quick. Because they were scared that the Russians were coming. And they were scared for the Russians still.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to leave the patients and went. And I was lucky enough to get in a truck again, to the station. And the cattle train was standing already. And all prisoners-- non-Jewish prisoners in the uniforms, striped jackets-- were unloaded out of the train. And we were put in, not clean, there were lice in already in the wagons. They were, outside, there were heaps of beetroot, raw beetroot, and kohlrabi, which is like sweet-- you know, when you put in soup, those white things. Sprouts?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. But they're raw, like carrots.

speaker: interviewer
text: Radishes, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, something like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Turnips?

speaker: interviewee
text: Turnips, like turnips, yeah. Heaps of them, and you could take that to take in the train. We could-- later on we got a piece of bread. That was it. And so we went with about, I don't know, about 75 I think, in one wagon, sitting on the floor. And I was still, coming out of the hospital, still to look after them. And we went riding. And every day, somebody died. People died, left, right, and center of me. The train would stop, and we would kind of throw them out, more or less. They were buried on the spot or left there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to go to the toilet, we were sitting at the side of the road, and the Germans just looking on with the gun on the shoulder. And we could hear-- I had my 25th birthday on  the train. Don't ask me, did I have a calendar, did I know the date? I can't remember, but I knew it was my birthday. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sometimes the train had to stop because the airplanes were going over. At one stop we put something, a sheet or something, on the train on top, with a Star of David on it, you know, so they would see it. And we could hear shooting, that side and that side. You know, the Americans and the Russians. We did not know that our train was destined to have an accident in the River Elbe. That was the plan. But seeing that the Americans and the Russians came close, the guards got a bit scared and were a bit friendly towards us. So the accident did not happen. And one morning, knock knock at the door. Open up, open up, we're free. The Russians are here. So we all went to the village. I was not so lucky to get-- we had to go into the homes of the Germans, and me and two other--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go into that, can I just ask you, so over this time, you didn't know what was being, what was happening, where you were being taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we did not know we were in the River Elbe. To another camp, that was all we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the change in how the guards treated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't know this much, but there was one wagon with the leaders in it. Like I gave you a piece of paper that would say that it was in the train, that it was signed by a couple of people. Well, they were in the passenger's wagon, keeping-- because I had to go every day when somebody died, I had to go to that compartment and say, such and such died, such and such died, so that they could keep the lists, which was a good thing, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Jewish ladies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the matron of the-- who was matron in the hospital. She was there. And, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So the guards, then, all they did was decide not to kill you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't notice anything with the guards. Maybe the ones in the-- you know, the leaders might have had contact with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And so this time for you, was it a time of fear, or how were you feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were free. We were free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before that, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the train?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You know, there was one woman with two grown up children-- well, grown up, maybe 12, 13 years-- and her husband died. She was whole night, she was sitting there next to her dead husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in your carriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for you at this time, was it just a very confusing, frightening period?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So when you're actually told that you were free, what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had my locket in there, and I left it there. Forgot it all. Never saw it again. And we had to go to the houses of the Germans. Well, I wasn't that active, and the people I was with, you know, we took it easy. And by the time we came in the village, most of the houses were taken. And we found a house and just put a mattress on the floor. And some people got nice bedrooms. The cellars of those houses were full of food, bottled-- you know, bottled food. They had plenty.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we lived in that house until I got typhoid again, a different kind of typhoid. Everybody got typhoid again. Lots of people died, couldn't-- the food, they couldn't stand the food. Stomach wasn't used to it. They died. Were you with anybody who died?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And the Russians opened up a hospital. But somehow, I didn't go to that hospital. Then I got a little bit better. And I could walk around. And then one morning, the Russians are coming to shave our heads, you know, because of the lice. So the Russians came in the front door of our house, and I went out the back. And I walked and walked and walked all day. I didn't want my head shaven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your appearance important to you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's something, you know, I don't want a shaven head. And then there was somebody who, for a bit of food, he would clean your head. A special comb. I think it was my comb, but I lost it. It was a special comb. And I went there and she cleaned my head.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when--

speaker: interviewee
text: And right away, when we came in that town, and we went walking, walking, and there was somebody coming on a bike. And it was the first Russian coming in, in the village. And he was Jewish. The first, on the bike.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he couldn't understand that we didn't speak Yiddish. You know Yiddish? You've heard of that? But in Holland we didn't. And he couldn't understand being Jewish and not speaking Yiddish. And we was the forerunner. The forerunner to the whole Russian troops?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. It was the Russians that liberated you, though, wasn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But there were different troops.

speaker: interviewee
text: Different group. That was in the village. The Russians that was on the way, in the train. But that was the village, Trobitz. And now they call it the Lost Transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: This group that you were traveling with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the train, the Lost Train, the Lost Transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they call it that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Because we went without any purpose, driving around and around for two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if I can just ask you, when you went into the German houses, the Germans were still living there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no, they had to go out. They fled. And we got rations off the Russians. And they didn't-- didn't do anything to the Germans. No special treatment, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So the whole village was just abandoned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Troebitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: T-R-O-E-B-I-T-Z, Troebitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, so then you got sick again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I got better again. The doctor came a few times, yes. And I got better. And when they opened the hospital, and we had a little boy living with us. And he got sick. And I carried him to the truck to go to the hospital. And also after I got better, we had to peel potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We might just have to leave it here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. That's the end of Tape Three of the interview with Charlotte Melkman. It's the 24th of March, 1995. We are in Sydney, Australia.
